# Si doped CoO nanorods as peroxidase mimics for colorimetric sensing of reduced glutathione


Chunqiao Jin, Jiajia Lian, Yan Gao, Kangkang Guo, Kaili Wu, Linna Gao, Xianxi Zhang, Xiao Zhang, and Qing-Yun Liu

ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng. , Just Accepted Manuscript · DOI: 10.1021/acssuschemeng.9b02459 · Publication Date (Web): 29Jul2019

Downloaded from pubs.acs.org on July30, 2019


# Si doped CoO nanorods as peroxidase mimics for colorimetric sensing of reduced glutathione


Chunqiao Jin a , Jiajia Lian a , Yan Gao a , Kangkang Guo d , Kaili Wu a , Linna Gao* ,a Xianxi Zhang b , Xiao Zhang c and Qingyun Liu* ,a

- a College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Mining Disaster Prevention and Control Co-founded by Shandong Province and the Ministry of Science and Technology, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao266590, China

- b Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory/Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Energy Storage & Novel Cell Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng252000, China

- c College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Shandong Key Laboratory of Biochemical Analysis, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao266042, China

d College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan250100, China


# Corresponding Author


- E-mail: Qingyun Liu*: qyliu@sdust.edu.cn;

Linna Gao*: gaolinna78@sdust.edu.cn

ABSTRACT: Si doped CoO nanorods (denoted as Si-CoO) were prepared by the hydrothermal method. The Si-CoO composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), etc. The results suggest that Si element uniformly distributed onto CoO nanorods with a diameter of50-100nm. The as-prepared Si-CoO nanocomposite possessed an excellent peroxidase-like activity as well as quickly catalyzed the colorless substrate3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to be oxidized into blue oxTMB by H2O2only in20s, which was easily visually observed. The electron spin resonance (ESR) data confirms that superoxide radicals ( · O2 ) play a key function in catalytic reaction. On the basis of the peroxidase biomimetic activity of Si-CoO, we constructed a new biosensor for detection of H2O2and reduced glutathione (GSH) in the range of1-100uM with a limit of0.22and0.45uM, respectively.

KEYWORDS: SiO2; CoO; GSH; Peroxidase; Biosensor


# INTRODUCTION


Natural enzymes, especially horseradish peroxidase (HRP), have been widely used for detecting biomacromolecule due to their excellent selectivity and sensitivity. 1-2However, the high-cost and low stability in rigorous conditions obviously limited their industrial application. 3Thus, a large number of cheap, stable and efficient artificial nanozymes have been exploited. Among these candidates, metal oxide-based mimics have been focused on, such as MoO3/C, 4CoO, 5MnO2, 6NiO, 7V2O3 8and CuO9 , etc. With the development of peroxidase mimics, scientists further prepared composites to substitute natural peroxidases, including porphyrin modified oxides, 10-11Porphyrin-based porous organic frameworks, 12-13perylene diimide functionalized oxides, 14some polymer-based nanomaterials, 15and noble composites, 16etc. Comparied with natural enzymes, artificial nanomimics exhibit more stability in harsh conditions (pH and temperature), and easy preparation. 17Nevertheless, metal oxides as biomimetic nanoenzymes exhibited some disadvantages, including not only low stability and anti-poisoning ability, but also easier accumulation in solution, 18significantly resulting in decreasing the activity of the nanoenzymes. Therefore, how to improve the catalytic performance of the artificial mimics is an urgent task.

Recently, as a typical p-type semiconductor, cobaltous oxide (CoO) with different morphologies, is widely used in lithium storage, catalysts, gas sensors, and magnetic materials, etc., 19-22due to its excellent electron transfer ability. 23-24Nevertheless, CoO nanoparticles were inevitably apt to aggregation, which restricted

its catalytic performance. To solve the aggregation of nanoparticles, some supports have been employed to prepare nanocomposites, 25-26as well as coating on the surface of nanoparticles to improve the dispersity of nanoparticles in water. However, as far as the later concerned, the catalytic activity of the coated nanoparticles by silicon27or polymer28was dramatically decreased, due to reducing of active sites. Thus, doping is a popular method which can not only solve the aggregation of nanopartciles but also retain activity sites on the surface of nanoparticles. Moreover, the solubility of Si doped nano-oxides was greatly enhanced, 29which was very important in study of biological fields. Based on the ideas on my mind, if CoO nanorods is doped with silicon in the process of preparation, the obtained Si doped CoO nanoparticles as peroxidase mimics will exhibit a highly catalytic activity. What's more, rich oxygen vacancies greatly improved the catalytic activity of Si-CoO.

Herein, Si doped CoO nanorods (Si-CoO) were prepared by a hydrothermal method. The CoO nanorods doped with silicon possessed much excellent peroxidase performance, compared with that of pure CoO. The enhanced catalytic activity of Si-CoO is attributed to increasing of oxygen vacancy. Based on the peroxdase-like activity of Si-CoO, a colorimetric method was constructed for highly sensitive determination of H2O2and glutathione (GSH) with high selectivity, respectively. ESR data reveal the catalytic mechanism is from superoxide radicals ( · O2 -).


# EXPERIMENTALSECTION


Reagents and Materials. Co(NO3)·6H2O, urea, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), H2O2

(30wt%), various metal salts, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine dihydrochloride (TMB·2HCl) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. D-Leucine (Leu), Urio acid (UA), DL-ISOLeucine (ISO), L-Arginine (Arg), DL-Tryptophan (Try), D-Histidine (His) and glutathione were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. (Saint Louis, USA). The medical GSH pills (93.34%) was produced by Shandong Luye Pharmaceutical Co. All chemicals were analytical. Ultra-pure water was from Lichun water purification system.

Apparatus. The morphology of Si-CoO nanorods were systematically investigated by FE-SEM (BRUKER XFlash6100), TEM and HRTEM (JEOL JEM-2100F) and the elemental images were detected by using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). XRD patterns of the samples were obtained from a Rigaku X-ray diffractimeter with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406 Å), and the2θ range varying from10o to80o . The elementary valence state and composition were revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a PHI Quantera SXM spectrometer with an Al Kα radiator (Thermo escalab250XI, USA). The absorbance was recorded on UV-1810PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). The production of O2radical was detected by electron spin resonance (ESR) on a Bruker ESR A200spectrometer with DMPO as the spin trap. Heat treatment was conducted in a tube furnace with a quartz tube (OTF-1200X, China). Contact angles were detected by DSA100 (Kruss, Germany).

Synthesis of Si-CoO. Si-CoO nanorods were prepared based on the publication with a minor modification. 30Cobalt nitrate (0.6926g), urea (0.6g) and deionized water (50mL) with different volume TEOS (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300and400uL) were mixed thoroughly, transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves (100mL) at100o C for3h. Then, the raw products were centrifuged and washed with pure water and ethanol in turn for5times. After that, the raw products were heated in nitrogen atmosphere from20to500o C and heating at500 ℃ for2h with a ramp rate of5o Cmin -1 .


# Peroxidase-like activity and steady-state kinetic assay of Si-CoO


The peroxidase-like activity of Si-CoO was evaluated by catalyzing the oxidation of the chromogenic substrate TMB by H2O2. The maximum absorbance of oxTMB was tested at652nm. 31Typically, 0.3mg mL -1of Si-CoO was mixed in acetate buffer solution (1.4mL, pH5.0), followed by adding of200uL of1mM TMB solution and200uL of0.25M H2O2. After3min, the absorbance of reaction systems were measured by the UV-vis absorption spectroscopy. All the reactions were processed at room temperature under the conditions mentioned above unless otherwise stated.

The kinetic test of Si-CoO was performed by varying concentration of TMB while keeping H2O2concentration unchanged and vice versa. A series of initial reaction rates were calculated according to the Michaelis-Menten equation, 1/v= (Km /Vmax) × (1/[S]) + 1/Vmax, where Km is the Michaelis constant, v is the rate of conversion, Vmax is the maximum rate of conversion, and [S] is the substrate

concentration.


# Detection of H2O2and GSH


Based on the peroxidase performance of Si-CoO, a colorimetric detection platform was designed for detecting H2O2and GSH, respectively. The typical procedure of H2O2detection is as follows: 200uL of0.3mg/mL Si-CoO, 200uL H2O2with varying concentrations and200uL of1mM TMB were incubated into buffer solution (1.4mL, pH=5) and then tested the absorbance of the mixture after3min. The detection of GSH is as follows: It took3min for the mixed solution containing200uL of0.3mg/mL Si-CoO, 200uL H2O2, 200uL of1mM TMB and1.4mL acetate buffer solution (pH=5) to react. Immediately, add200uL of GSH with different concentrations into the above mixture, and measure on a UV-vis spectrophotometer.


# Active species capturing.


The superoxide radicals (·O2 -), holes (h + ) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) were probed by using sacrificial agents such as P-Benzoquinone (PBQ), 2-propanol (IPA) and disodium ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA). This method is similar to detection of H2O2, sacrificial agents are added to reaction systems before to add H2O2, respectively. Furthermore, ·O2and ·OH are captured by the5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) which can be devoted to detect radicals by ESR technique. Before determining two kinds of radicals (DMPO-·O2and DMPO-·OH), 3mg·Si-CoO, 1mL H2O2and240uL DMPO were dissolve in10mL methanol or DI water and reacted for3min.


# RESULTSAND DISCUSSION



# Characterization of Si-CoO


Fig. 1shows the XRD of pure CoO and Si-CoO, respectively. The peaks appeared at2θ = 36.503o , 42.401o , 61.520o , 73.700o and77.562o are corresponding to the crystal faces (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) of CoO (JCPDS43-1004). Notably, it is obviously that the peaks of Si-CoO are consistent with that of pure CoO, suggesting that the crystal form of CoO in the silicon doped CoO nanorods is not changed.

Fig. 1XRD patterns of CoO (a) and Si-CoO (b), respectively.

Furthermore, Fig. 2shows XPS data can be confirmed the existence of Si element in the nanocomposite. The survey spectrum ( Fig. 2a) shows that Si-CoO contain Si, O, and Co elements. Besides, Fig. 2b displays that two typical satellite peaks at797.6and780.9eV are attributed to the Co2+ . As shown in Fig. 2c, it can be

seen that one peak at102.7eV is attributed to Si2p, similar to the reported result of SiO2 (103.4eV). 32The little change of binding energy is caused by the higher electronegativity of Si in comparison with Co, indicating the existence of Co-O-Si bond. Moreover, as can be found in Fig. 2d, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s shows three peaks (O1, O2and O3) by deconvolution. The O1peak at532.5eV is assigned to the lattice oxygen atoms of Si-O-Si bond, and the peak at529.7eV (O3) corresponds to the metal oxygen bond of Co-O-Co. Notably, the peak at531.5eV (O2) is attributed to oxygen defects from CoO. 33Futhermore, compared with that in Fig. S2 (supporting information), the peak area for oxygen defects is obviously increased after doping Si, which is very important in enhancing the excellent catalytic activity of silicon doped CoO in the subsequent experiments. Based on the data described above, it is verified that the Si-CoO has been successfully prepared. 34-35

Fig. 2XPS spectra of Si-CoO: survey spectra (a), Co2p (b), Si2p (c), and O1s (d), respectively.

Fig. 3shows the morphology of the Si-CoO imaged by TEM and SEM, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 3a and3c, it can be found that the Si-CoO nanorods with a width of50-100nm, are composed with lots of nanoparticles with ca. 15nm in size. From Fig. 3b, it is clearly seen that the lattice fringe with ca. 0.21nm in d-spacing, corresponds to the (111) lattice plane of CoO. 19, 36Compared with pure CoO (Fig. S3, supporting information), the mophology of nanocomposites is negligible change, although silicon element is introduced in the process of preparation. Furthermore, the Co, Si and O are detected in the nanocmposites, confirming the formation of Si-CoO.

Fig. 3TEM (a), HRTEM (b), SEM images (c) and EDX mappings (d) of Si-CoO, respectively.

Generally, good surface wettability facilitates the dispersion in solution as well as the increase of the catalytic activity. 37The contact angles were tested to compare the hydrophilic property of CoO nanorods with and without Si doping. As shown in Fig. 4 , the contact angles of CoO and Si-CoO are37.5° and27.6°, respectively. It is suggested that the hydrophilic performance of Si-CoO is developed, due to the doping of Si.

Fig. 4Hydrophilic performance of CoO (a) and Si-CoO (b), respectively.

Fig. S1shows UV-vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) of CoO and Si-CoO, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1a, pure CoO (curve a) exhibits a significant absorption from ultraviolet to visible light region, while Si-CoO (red line) shows a slight weak. According to the Rauc plot equation, the band gap energy of Si-CoO is calculated to be2.43eV, similar to that of pure CoO ( Fig. S1b), consistent with the previous results. 38Combined with XPS data, it is demonstrated that Si doping changes the electronic structure of CoO and thereby improves the separation and transportation photoexcited charge carries. 30


# Peroxidase-like activity of Si-CoO.


To investigate the peroxidase mimetic activity of Si-CoO, TMB is chosen as chromogenic substrate in the oxidation reaction, displayed in Fig. 5 . As shown in the figure, no obvious color change is distinguished in the absence (curve a and c) and presence (curve b) of H2O2before adding Si-CoO into solutions. Notably, TMB isn't also oxidized without H2O2, though Si-CoO is added into the reaction system, indicating no oxidase-like activity of Si-CoO (curve d). From curve e in Fig. 5, it is clearly seen that the TMB is easily oxidized in the presence of H2O2, suggesting that pure CoO possesses the peroxidase-like activity. Furthermore, from curve f, a much more obvious blue change is quickly observed, once Si-CoO is added in the H2O2/TMB system, verifying that the catalytic activity of Si-CoO is greatly improved, owing to Si doping. In fact, after Si-CoO was added into the TMB-H2O2system for ca. 10s, an obvious blue change was observed visually, suggesting the fast oxidation of

TMB into oxTMB catalyzed by Si-CoO. 39In brief, the peroxidase-like activity of Si-CoO is dramatically increased, compared with that of CoO.

Fig. 5Adsorption spectra and color changes (3min) of different systems: TMB (a), TMB + H2O2 (b), TMB + CoO (c), TMB + Si-CoO (d), TMB + H2O2 + CoO (e), and TMB + H2O2 + Si-CoO (f), respectively.


# Influences of temperature, pH and the amount of TEOS


Similar to the natural peroxidase (HRP) and some reported artificial peroxidase mimics, 40-41temperature, pH together with the amount of TEOS obviously affect the catalytic performance of Si-CoO. As can be seen from Fig. 6a, the relative activity of Si-CoO quickly increases and then drastically reduces in the pH range from2to7. As a result, the maximum of catalytic activity of Si-CoO achieves at pH5. Thus, the weaker acidic environment is in favor of the catalytic activity of Si-CoO. As we know,

H2O2is easily decomposed into H2O and O2under the basic condition, causing the dramatic decrease of the amount of H2O2and further resulting in the reduction of the catalytic performance of the composites in the reaction system. Obviously, as displayed in Fig. 6b, the Si-CoO kept high activity (>80%) in the temperature range of15 -40o C, just fitting with the ambient temperature together with the body temperature, which is critical to apply Si-CoO in fields of environment and biology. Besides, the catalytic activity of Si-CoO reaches the maximum when the amount of TEOS is150 μL. In general, the doped Si is in the form of SiO2with no catalytic performance. However, it is found that the moderate amount of TEOS develops the catalytic activity of CoO, owing to increasing of oxygen vacancy together with the water solubility. As the concentration of TEOS increasing, the catalytic activity of Si-CoO is reduced, owing to the absence of active sites in Si-CoO nanocomposites. Based on these results, the temperature25o C and pH5are selected for the subsequent experiments.

Fig. 6Optimization of experimental conditions: the pH of the reaction buffer from2to7 (a), the temperature from10to45o C (b), and the amount of TEOS from50to400uL (c), respectively.


# Steady-state kinetic analysis of Si-CoO.


To further investigate the peroxidase-like activity of Si-CoO, kinetic assay was performed. Fig. 7shows that the catalytic behaviors are consistent with the typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics and double-reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plots (inset of Fig. 7a and7b) using TMB and H2O2as the substrates. As shown in Table1, the value of Km and Vmax for Si-CoO with TMB as the substrate are0.0589mM and2.50×10 -7Ms -1 , respectively. For comparative investigates, some previous report results, such as Co3O4, SiO2@Co3O4and Co3O4@CeO2are listed in Table1. 42-45Notably, a significant lower or similar Km of the Si-CoO, implies that Si-CoO has a great affinity with TMB. On the other hand, the value of Km and Vmax for Si-CoO with H2O2are0.023mM and1.04×10 -8Ms -1 , lower than that of Co3O4 (140.07mM), 42Co3O4 (173.51mM), 43SiO2@ Co3O4 (25.2mM) 44and Co3O4@CeO2 (7.09mM), 45indicating that less H2O2is required to achieve catalytic activity. To comparative study of the catalytic performance of mimic enzymes, kcat/Km value were also estimated. The larger the kcat/Km value, the stronger the peroxidase-like activity of mimic enzymes.

From the data listed in Table1, kcat/Km value of Si-CoO is higher than that of Co3O4and SiO2@Co3O4. As a result, Si-CoO exhibits much better catalytic activity.

Fig. 7Steady-state kinetic assay of Si-CoO by varying the concentration of TMB (20-100mM) with a fixed H2O2 (0.25M) (a), and tuning the concentration of H2O2 (10-100μM) with a fixed TMB (1mM) (b), respectively. The insets represent the double-reciprocal model of TMB and H2O2.

Table1Kinetic parameters for the oxidation of substrates by different catalysts.

*[E] is the concentration of catalyst (e.g. Si-CoO), and kcat is the catalytic constant, where kcat =

Vmax/ [E].


# Detection of H2O2and GSH using Si-CoO as enzyme mimic.


H2O2is one of the small molecule reactive oxygen species (ROS) in living organisms and a universal mark of oxidative stess. 46,28Therefore, the detection of H2O2is an important task. Based on the catalytic performance of Si-CoO as peroxidase mimic, convenient and ultrasensitive colorimetric detection systems of H2O2and GSH are designed without using sophisticated instruments. Fig. 8 . shows the response curves of the typical absorbance at652nm with various concentration of H2O2and GSH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8a, the linear range of H2O2concentration is in the range of2-10uM with the limit of detection (LOD) of4.32×10 -6M.

As a almighty protector, GSH plays a significance role for some protective enzymes including GSH peroxidase, glyoxalase and GSH S-transferases. 47Besides, GSH can also protect cells against electrophiles and oxidative damage due to ROS that related to numerous clinical diseases (e.g., HIV, cancer, diabetes, Alzheimer's disease). 48-50Therefore, it's meaningful to fast and accurate to detect GSH. 51-52GSH reacted with oxTMB and H2O2, which caused the fading blue color, owing to its an excellent reducibility. As shown in Fig. 8b, the ΔA (ΔA = Ablank - AGSH) increases with increasing of concentration of GSH, suggesting that the blue oxTMB was gradually changed to colorless TMB. It can be obtained that linear response of GSH is in the range of1-5uM, with a detection limit of0.45 × 10 -6M lower than that of

Co-MOCP/CPE, Probe1, NiS/MMT/GO, and MoS2/rGO, reported in Table2 . 53-56

Fig. 8The linear calibration plots for H2O2 (a) and GSH (b) based on the catalytic activity of Si-CoO as peroxidase mimic with UV-vis absorption at652nm, respectively.


# Selectivity of detection of GSH.


Selectivity of the proposed sensor based on Si-CoO is important for GSH detection. Thus, a series of control experiments are designed and implemented, including amino acids and various metal ions. From Fig. 9, even though the

concentration of interferents is10times higher than that of GSH, the colorimetric method for the detection of GSH exhibits a higher selectivity.

Fig. 9The selectivity of the GSH colorimetric sensor. The concentration of interferents is5mM.


# The stability and recyclability of Si-CoO.


The stability and recyclability of Si-CoO as artificial enzymes are important in practical application. Fig. 10a shows a slight decrease of peroxidase activity after stored for30d, indicating the good stability of Si-CoO. As shown in Fig. 10b , the activity of Si-CoO remained more than70% after reacted for5cycles. This slight decrease may be caused by the sample loss during each centrifugal treatment.

Time (d)

Fig. 10The stability (a) and recyclability (b) of catalytic of Si-CoO, respectively.


# Practical detection in real sample


To confirm the practicability of the colorimetric method based on Si-CoO as the peroxidase mimic, the amount of GSH in medical pills was determined. From Table S1, the recoveries of the medical samples range from96to106.7% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) as low as1.8%, indicating that the established sensor exhibits a high credibility for detection of GSH in real sample. Thus, a fast and sensitive detection of real samples was established.


# Catalytic mechanism of Si-CoO


The main active species formed in the Si-CoO/H2O2system were determined for investigating the catalytic mechanism of Si-CoO. Three sacrificial agents including P-Benzoquinone (PBQ), 2-propanol (IPA) and disodium ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) are used to capture · O2 -, · OH and h + , respectively. It is obviously that the decrease of relative activity of Si-CoO is along with adding of PBQ, suggesting that · O2plays a major role in the catalytic system ( Fig. 11 ).

Fig. 11Trapping experiments of active species during the catalysis of TMB over Si-CoO.

In addition, DMPO spin trapping ESR spectrum further confirms the generation of · O2and · OH in the Si-CoO/H2O2system. As shown in Fig. 12 , when the Si-CoO/H2O2system is exposed in the visible light, the signal intensity is stronger than that in dark. And the signal of · O2is more obvious than that of ·OH either in dark or light ( Fig. 12 ). As a result, three active species co-existed in Si-CoO/H2O2system, and the · O2playes a major role in the catalytic activity of Si-CoO. According to the previous report, 56the photogenerated charge carriers migrate to the surface of Si-CoO with a reduced recombination rate with holes and then a series of redox reactions occur at the solid-liquid interface. Based on the above results, the main reactions involved in the catalytic system can be concluded as follows:

O2 , h + , · OH + TMB → oxTMB (5)

The catalytic mechanism of Si-CoO as a peroxidase mimic is illustrated in Scheme1 .

Fig. 12DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra for Si-CoO in methanol dispersion for DMPO·O2 (a) and aqueous dispersion for DMPO·OH (b), respectively.

Scheme1The catalytic mechanism of Si-CoO.


# CONCLUSION


In summary, silicon doped CoO nanorods was successfully prepared and confirmed to possess the enhanced peroxidase-like activity. The superior catalytic activity of Si-CoO are ascribed to co-existence of ·O2 -, ·OH and h + in the TMB/H2O2/Si-CoO reaction system. Based on the excellent peroxidase-like activity, stability and reusability of Si-CoO, a highly sensitive and selective colorimetric sensor was established for convenient detection of GSH with0.45uM of LOD. It is anticipated that our work will facilitate Si-CoO as the peroxidase mimic to be applied in bio-sensing, food and environment detection.
